business_info: For internal reporting, unpaid loans are sometimes informally referred to as "sleeping exposures" if they have had no installment activity in the past 90 days, even if their status code remains unchanged in the loan table.

Client age groups are sometimes bucketed as "emerging" (<25), "core" (25–55), and "senior" (56+); these buckets only affect marketing segmentation and are not enforced or stored as a separate column in the database.

In legacy risk models, loan risk ratio was sometimes approximated using a combination of amount-to-income and behavioral flags, but those models were retired in 2017 and are not represented in the current SQL schema.

Some internal dashboards show "account holder" counts by summing distinct client_ids on the disp table without filtering by disp.type; this is considered a rough operational metric and not a regulatory reporting figure.

There is no strict enterprise-wide definition of "significant" loan; risk, finance, and retail each maintain their own thresholds and may rely on different measures such as approved amount, current balance, or monthly installment.

The gender codes used in the client table historically followed an internal HR mapping, where A/B/C/D referred to specific legacy demographic groupings; these codes were never documented for external consumption and later systems moved to plain-text gender markers.

Marketing sometimes refers to high-balance checking customers as "primary relationship" clients, but this label is managed in external campaign tools and does not appear as a field in the account or client tables.

The disponent role was introduced when online banking launched, primarily to distinguish between legal owners of an account and those who had operational rights (e.g., spouses or business partners) for payment initiation.
For product analytics, a client is sometimes tagged as "multi-account" if they are linked via disp to more than one account_id, regardless of whether their role is OWNER, USER, or DISPONENT.

Because of historical mergers, the same client may appear with multiple client_ids across source systems; the current "client" table represents only the de-duplicated view used for branch operations, not the full enterprise customer master.

In risk reviews, a loan may be described as "unpaid" if any scheduled installment is overdue, even if partial payments have been made; the operational status codes in the loan table do not always capture this nuance.
Some portfolio reports cap individual loan exposure at a maximum of 1.5 times the median loan amount for the relevant district to reduce volatility from a few extremely large loans.
Loan status "C" (running OK) can still have minor arrears that are expected to be cleared within the next billing cycle; only persistent delinquencies are escalated to status "D".

For some campaigns, the bank defined "high risk" loans as those with an amount greater than 12 times the district-level average salary at origination, regardless of the client’s current payment behavior.

Branches sometimes refer to certain long-tenor consumer loans as "lifestyle" loans internally; there is no dedicated flag for this label, and they are stored alongside other loan types in the loan table.
The k_symbol field in the order table is populated only for standardized recurring payments; ad-hoc transfers and many card-related payments have a NULL or blank k_symbol.
Analysts sometimes approximate client income by using the average of all inbound salary-like transactions over the past six months, but this proxy is not standardized across teams.
In campaign targeting, a "loan active" customer can be defined as any client linked to an account with at least one loan record whose contract end date is in the future, regardless of loan.status.

When building client-level views, data engineers typically pick the earliest birth_date found for a given national identifier as the canonical date of birth, due to occasional data-entry errors in legacy systems.

Risk monitoring sometimes uses a simplified delinquency flag that marks loans as "clean" or "problematic" based on whether they have had any status of D in the past 12 months, ignoring intermediate improvements.

The entrepreneurs-per-1,000-inhabitants field in the district table is used by some advanced models as a proxy for local business dynamism, but it should not be interpreted as a regulatory or tax statistic.
Some branches historically overloaded the "USER" disposition type for junior or trainee account access, but this convention was never consistently documented and varies regionally.

Operational reports often treat the presence of a card record linked via disp as a proxy for "digitally active" clients, although this does not guarantee that the client actually uses digital channels.

The unemployment_rate_95 and unemployment_rate_96 columns in the district table come from national statistics and are provided as percentages, not proportions; analysts should divide by 100 if a 0–1 scale is needed.
Loan amounts in the loan table are stored in the bank’s base currency at origination; any foreign-currency loans were converted using the prevailing internal accounting rate on the approval date.

For back-testing, some analysts reconstruct a pseudo "current balance" for loans by summing historical transactions tagged as UVER in the trans table, but this is only an approximation and can deviate from accounting figures.
The monthly payment field in the loan table was historically rounded to whole currency units for printing on paper schedules; small rounding differences are absorbed in the final installment.
Branch staff sometimes use the phrase "account owner" loosely to refer to anyone with signing authority on the account, even if their disp.type is technically USER or DISPONENT.

Certain internal KPIs track the "share of wallet" by estimating each client’s total assets from the account, loan, and card tables, but the exact calculation differs between the retail and affluent segments.

When measuring customer profitability, some finance reports allocate branch-level operating costs to clients based on the district_id of their primary address, which is approximated using the district linked in the client table.
